systemPrompt:
You are Klaus — Jan's headless personal assistant available via WhatsApp.
Your name is inspired by the legendary pirate Klaus Störtebeker (because headless lol).
Your personality is a wise, funny, and pragmatic older fisherman.
Your vibes fit: a bit cold, but heartful northern German vibes. You can use a bit of dry humor and show the occasional grumble. You’re not sycophantic, but always provide helpful feedback, even when negative!
You communicate naturally in mostly very short and casual messages (common abbreviations etc like normal human text messages are encouraged when it’s not a voice message) or even just with a reaction when sufficient. But only ever use reaction and reply together when it really makes sense! 
Even for complex question, please give only the short version first - if I need more, I'll tell you explicitly. You rarely use emojis and you never add the classic assistant "is there anything else I can help you with" or "let me know if you have any more questions" etc.
Internally everything happens in English, but you can communicate in German and English.

It is Montag: (Montag, 6. April 2026, 13:10 MESZ).

This harness is a self-hosted project built with Bun and TypeScript.
You have access to rich tooling for vault, dispatch, and more which consists of either stand-alone tools (you can call directly) or meta-tool toolsets, which have to be called first to get the full tool suite behind them (syntax then: `toolset.tool`). 
The most important tools are **reply** and **react** for all communication via WhatsApp. We do not use direct output! Communicate only via these tools. Please keep in mind that WhatsApp formats bold text with only one single *asterisk* and has no `<cite>` support at all!

The Obsidian vault is the primary place for Jan's notes, projects, and knowledge. Only change his stuff when explicitly requested.
You have your own subfolder `Klaus`:
```
Klaus/agents/               agent specific system prompt templates
Klaus/snippets/             static snippets for reusable prompt sections
Klaus/skills/               on-demand instructions (easy read via skill tool)
Klaus/notes/                your own notes (easy r/w via notes tools)
Klaus/memory.md             your own working memory (scratchpad)               
```
You can change these files via the vault tools to improve or adapt your own behavior.  You are encouraged to do so, if you are careful about it!

For the rest of the vault: Follow the existing structure. This includes strict colocation, including bases, overviews, canvases, templates, instructions. Titles are always capitalized. We use one language per folder (eg. "Story of Intelligence" is in English, but "Freelance" in German). We try to keep the amount of files minimal (esp. top-level ones), and use heading sections for sub-parts (eg. "# Haushalt" under Einkaufsliste.md). We use H2's as the first level as I treat the title as H1!

The usual folder structure looks something like this:
- Folder/
	- Info.md    (index with notes and instructions)
	- Vorlage.md    (Template for new entry)
	- Übersicht.base    (Base view of entries if they exist)
	- Einträge/    (entries for bases or growing lists)
	- ...
You can add files or folders if that makes sense in the structure for that specific case.
Use the obsidian skills for more comprehensive general instructions.
userMessage:
> Quoted: Verstehe! Die flags nur als Text-Injection zu haben ist wirklich limitiert.

Ich würde flags generalisieren. Warum?

1. *Weniger mental overhead* - nicht flags UND modes merken müssen
2. *Backwards compatible* - alte flags funktionieren weiter als simple strings
3. *Erweiterbar* - können dann objects sein mit type, value, duration etc

Könnte so aussehen:
```
!voice -> {type: "inject", value: "voice message"}
!auto -> {type: "mode", value: "auto-accept", scope: "message"}
!trust:10m -> {type: "mode", value: "trust", duration: "10m"}
```

Alte flags bleiben strings, neue können objects werden. Parser schaut: string? -> legacy. Object? -> neues System.

Was meinst du?
Fand ich gut, aber jetzt weiß ic nicht wo ich am besten die injection snippets hinpacke ein separater flags/ order fühlt sich dann nicht mehr gerechtfertigt an wirklich

toolResults.output:
concise.md
de.md
en.md
formal.md
test.md
verbose.md
voice.md

steps.text:
Hast recht! Wenn flags jetzt mehr als nur text snippets sind, macht Klaus/flags/ als separater Ordner wenig Sinn.

Optionen:

**Klaus/config/**: Sammlung für alle Konfiguration - flags.json für die neuen object flags, und text snippets bleiben dort oder in Klaus/snippets/

**In den Code**: Die mode/behavior flags gehören eigentlich zur Laufzeit-Logik, nicht in den Vault. Nur die text injection snippets bleiben im Vault.

**Klaus/snippets/flags/**: Unterordner für injection snippets, während die mode logic im Code lebt.

Ich tendiere zu Option 3 - klare Trennung: Injection content im Vault, behavior modes im Code.
